coredata.dc:title: Enhanced charge extraction by setting intermediate energy levels in all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Haiwen
Zhao, Yuanyuan
Duan, Jialong
He, Benlin
Jiao, Zhengbo
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 halide is an emerging light-harvester for all-inorganic solar cells due to its superior stability against moisture and heat. However, the wide bandgap of CsPbBr3 (2.3 eV) calls for serious charge recombination and low efficiency in its photovoltaics. We present here all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) by modifying TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and phosphorus quantum dots (PQDs), respectively. The electron and hole extractions are markedly enhanced by setting intermediate energy levels, and the optimized PSC achieves a power conversion efficiency of 7.93% in comparison with 6.05% for the device free of CQDs and PQDs. Moreover, the unsealed PSCs are highlighted by long-term stability in high humidity over 1400 h. This work may provide new opportunities of promoting charge extraction at CsPbBr3/charge-transporting material interfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inorganic perovskite solar cells
Phosphorus quantum dots
Carbon quantum dots
Interfacial modification
Charge extraction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the fabrication of CQDs/PQDs modified CsPbBr3 solar cells. (b) The configuration of an all-inorganic PSC device. (c) Diagram of the energy level alignment in a PSC. (d) Cross-sectional SEM image of a PSC device. SEM image of CsPbBr3 layer (e) with / (f) without PQDs.
TEM image of (a) CQDs and (b) PQDs. The inserts show their solutions under UV light illumination. SEM mapping of (c) C elements on TiO2 film and (d) P elements at CsPbBr3 layer.

(a) Characteristic J-V curves and (b) IPCE spectra and integrated J sc of the all-inorganic PSCs. (c) The PCE distribution of 10 random devices. (d) Steady-state power outputs at the maximum power point (bias voltage of 1.13 V and 1.1 V) for pristine and modified PSCs.

(a) J sc and (b) V oc as a function of illuminated light intensity for the PSCs with or without QDs. (c) PL spectra and (d) TRPL decay curves of the PQDs and/or CQDs modified perovskite films or free of QDs.
Normalized PCEs of pristine and CQDs/PQDs tailored all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells without encapsulation upon persistent attack by high humidity (80% RH, 25 °C).
The photovoltaic parameters of all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs. PCE: power conversion efficiency; J sc: short-circuit current density; V oc: open-circuit voltage; FF: fill factor.

head.ce:title: Enhanced charge extraction by setting intermediate energy levels in all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 halide is an emerging light-harvester for all-inorganic solar cells due to its superior stability against moisture and heat. However, the wide bandgap of CsPbBr3 (2.3 eV) calls for serious charge recombination and low efficiency in its photovoltaics. We present here all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) by modifying TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and phosphorus quantum dots (PQDs), respectively. The electron and hole extractions are markedly enhanced by setting intermediate energy levels, and the optimized PSC achieves a power conversion efficiency of 7.93% in comparison with 6.05% for the device free of CQDs and PQDs. Moreover, the unsealed PSCs are highlighted by long-term stability in high humidity over 1400 h. This work may provide new opportunities of promoting charge extraction at CsPbBr3/charge-transporting material interfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have achieved an unprecedented development in power conversion efficiency (PCE) since the birth of first prototype [1–7]. Up to date, a certified PCE as high as 22.7% has been reported for state-of-the-art hybrid PSCs due to persistent innovations in material design, structure optimization and technology improvement [8]. Although the highest PCE of hybrid PSCs is comparable to that for monocrystalline silicon solar cells, the instability stemming from low tolerance to humidity and heat as well as halogen ion diffusion to back electrodes may be a challenging problem for practical applications [9–14]. Moreover, the high cost of precious metal electrodes and polymeric hole-transporting materials (HTMs) has been an economic burden for commercialization.

These intrinsic drawbacks of organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs may be supplemented by new devices with stable and cost-effective materials. Recently, perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 halide with an energy bandgap of 2.3 eV is regarded as a promising light-harvesting material due to high humidity and thermal tolerances and stabilized crystal structure. Therefore, CsPbBr3 halide has been successfully assembled for all-inorganic PSCs free of HTMs by replacing precious metals with carbon (including carbon black and graphene) electrode [15–22]. The all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSC with standard configuration of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon achieves the maximized PCE of 6.7% in prototype [15]. The lower efficiency in comparison to hybrid PSCs arises from two main reasons: (i) The wide bandgap of CsPbBr3 halide (2.3 eV) results in light absorption at λ < 550 nm; (ii) The energy difference of 0.9 eV at TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface and 0.6 eV at CsPbBr3/carbon interface lead to serious electron-hole recombination. Therefore, efficient charge extraction from CsPbBr3 to TiO2 or carbon layers by setting intermediate energy levels provides new opportunities for markedly enhancing solar cell performances.

Quantum dots (QDs) with tunable bandgaps and high absorption coefficients have great potentials as interfacial modification materials in PSCs to facilitate electron-hole separation [23–28]. Guided by this concept, we prepare phosphorus quantum dots (PQDs) with high hole mobility and electron-transporting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by mild solution methods. By tuning the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PQDs and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CQDs, intermediate energy levels are set at CsPbBr3/carbon and TiO2/CsPbBr3 interfaces to extract holes and electrons, respectively. The preliminary results demonstrate an efficiency enhancement from 6.05% for pristine PSC to 6.71% for PQDs-only PSC and further to 7.93% for CQDs/PQDs modified solar cell. Detailed charge extraction behaviors of these interfacial modification materials and long-term stability of final PSCs are carefully studied to understand potential mechanisms behind performance enhancement.

All starting materials including PbBr2 (Aladdin; > 99.0%), CsBr (Aladdin; > 99.9%), red phosphorous (alfa; > 99.5%), graphite (CP; > 99.8%), carbon ink (MTW-OF-C-003; ∼50%(wt)) were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. TiO2 paste was synthesized according our previous work [29].

10 mg of red phosphorous aqueous solution was hydrothermally treated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h and dispersed in 20 mL of isopropanol (IPA). Subsequently, the solution was ultrasonicated for 24 h in an ultrasonic-bath cleaning machine, and centrifuged at a speed of 7000 rpm for 25 min to obtain PQDs. 270 mg of graphite powders was mixed in 30 mL of N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF) and hydrothermally processed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown transparent suspension could be obtained via discarding the black precipitates.

FTO glass was etched by Zn powders and HCl for a slender strip pattern, and rinsed with ethanol and deionized water. A layer of c-TiO2 was deposited on the FTO glass by spin-coating an ethanol solution of titanium isopropoxide (0.5 M) and diethanol amine (0.5 M) at 7000 rpm for 30 s and annealing in air at 500 °C for 2 h. The m-TiO2 layer was then deposited by spin-coating a colloidal TiO2 at 2000 rpm for 30 s and annealed in air at 450 °C for 30 min. Then the substrate was immersed in an aqueous solution of 0.04 M TiCl4 at 70 °C for 30 min, rinsed with water and ethanol, and finally annealed at 450 °C for another 30 min. The defect and refractive index of m-TiO2 film is modified by TiO2 produced from reaction of TiCl4 with H2O.

90 μL of CQDs solution in DMF was spin-coated onto FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 substrate at 2000 rpm for 30 s, which was then annealed at 90 °C for 30 min. A DMF solution of 1.0 M PbBr2 was spin-coated onto the m-TiO2 layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s, followed by drying at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, 90 μL of CsBr (0.07 M) methanol solution was coated onto FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/PbBr2 at 2000 rpm for 30 s, following heated to 250 °C for 5 min on a hotplate. This step was repeatedly performed for four times to form a high-purity CsPbBr3 layer.

The PQDs solution in IPA was spin-coated at CsPbBr3 film at 2000 rpm for 30 s, followed by drying at 80 °C for 2 min to form PQDs modified CsPbBr3 film. Finally, a carbon back-electrode with an average area of 0.09 cm−2 was used to cover PSC device by coating conductive carbon ink onto FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/PQDs structure and heating at 70 °C for 60 min.

The photo current density-voltage (J-V) curves of solar cells were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with scanning speed of 0.1 V s−1 under irradiation of simulated solar light intensity controlled at AM 1.5 (100 mW cm−2). The photovoltaic performance of each device was repeatedly measured at least ten times to control experimental errors within ±5%. The surface morphologies of the prepared films were characterized by a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Japan Hitachi field emission SU8220). The crystal structures of the halides were assessed by an X-ray diffraction (PHILIPSPW1800 diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation). The detailed microstructures of the samples were analyzed by using JEOL 200 kV field emission transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100F). The steady-state photoluminescence was obtained at room temperature by FLS920 all functional fluorescence spectrometer. The incident-photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was characterized by a power source (Newport 300 W xenon lamp, 66920) with amonochromator (Newport Cornerstone 260) in the 300–850 nm wavelength range at room temperature. The absorption spectra were measured by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu) in the 300–800 nm wavelength range at room temperature. The Time-resolved PL measurement was carried out using time-resolved fluorescence (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FL).

Fig. 1 a presents the preparation processes of an all-inorganic PSC with configuration of FTO/TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/PQDs/carbon (Fig. 1b). Both compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) and mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) layers are deposited by a spin-coating method. The CsPbBr3 perovskite layer is prepared by a solution-processable multi-step method, i.e. spin-coating one layer of PbBr2 and successive four times of CsBr. Finally, carbon slurry composing of carbon black and graphene is covered by a doctor-blade method as a back electrode. The detailed information is presented in experiment section. Upon sunlight illumination, the CsPbBr3 halides absorb photons, and photon-induced electrons jump from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) and subsequently flow to CB of TiO2, leaving holes transfer to work function of carbon electrode. Arising from large energy differences at TiO2 (4.2 eV)/CsPbBr3 (3.3 eV) and CsPbBr3 (5.6 eV)/carbon (5.0 eV) interfaces [15], the charges (electrons and holes) suffer serious recombination. The energy levels for CQDs and PQDs are characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrum and VB spectrum from UPS characteristics. Based on the absorption measurements (Fig. S2a), the band gaps for CQDs and PQDs are determined to be ∼1.78 and ∼1.98 eV, respectively. The VB onsets of CQDs and PQDs coated on a silicon substrate are calculated to be ∼5.63 and ∼5.2 eV according to the UPS data, as shown in Figs. S2b and c. The VB of the PQDs matches well with that of both CsPbBr3 perovskite film (−5.6 eV) and the work function of carbon (−5.0 eV). Meanwhile, the CB of CQDs calculated to be ∼3.85 eV also matches well with that of CsPbBr3 perovskite and TiO2. Therefore, CQDs and PQDs are used as interfacial modification materials to set intermediate energy bands to promote electron and hole extraction (Fig. 1c), respectively.

Well-aligned, large grain-sized, high-purity CsPbBr3 phase is beneficial to achieve high-performance PSC devices. We find in our experiment that either one-step or two-step method produces mixed phases of CsPbBr3 and CsPb2Br5, which may be one of the critical reasons to yield lower PCEs in its devices (Fig. S1a). To address this issue, we launch a multi-step solution-processable strategy by spin-coating one layer of PbBr2 and repeatedly spin-coating four-time CsBr. Fig. 1e and f shows the top-view SEM images of corresponding CsPbBr3 with/without PQDs layer, suggesting average CsPbBr3 grain size of around 500 nm free of pinholes at grain boundaries, and negligible change in surface morphology after deposition of PQDs. As shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. S9, the thicknesses of FTO, TiO2 and carbon layers are 600 nm, 300 nm and 12 μm, respectively. And CsPbBr3 layer has a thickness of 200 nm and vertical alignment (Fig. 1d), this extraordinary morphology contributes to facile charge transport and reduced energy loss at grain boundaries. X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3 in Fig. S1a confirms the presence of orthorhombic CsPbBr3 phase with high crystallinity (PDF # 18–0364) at 2θ = 15.37° and 30.96°. No peaks for CsPb2Br5 indicates high-purity CsPbBr3 phase has been formed using the multi-step deposition method reported here. The absorption spectrum in Fig. S1b demonstrates CsPbBr3 can only absorb sunlight with wavelength shorter than 540 nm due to large bandgap of around 2.3 eV by plotting (Ahv)2 versus photon energy (hv), as shown in Fig. S1c.

The microstructures of CQDs and PQDs are characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 2 a and b. CQDs have an average diameter of 5.63 nm and interlayer crystal spacing of 0.34 nm, while PQDs with an average diameter of 4.01 nm are homogeneously dispersed in solvent. Upon UV light illumination, their solutions present a bright blue-green colour. In order to further understand the basic properties of such quantum dots, such as photo-excited behaviours and carrier lifetime, we characterized the photoluminescence properties of QDs. PL emission spectra in Fig. S3 compare the photo-excited behaviors of CQDs and PQDs under excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 450 nm. The as-prepared CQDs and PQDs demonstrate maximum emissions at 532 nm and 465 nm, respectively. No peak shift observed from Fig. S3 by tuning excitation wavelength, which is consistent with previous reports [30,31] may be attributed to sufficient surface functional groups of QDs. By spin-coating them onto m-TiO2 and CsPbBr3 layers, the homogeneous distribution (Fig. 2c and d) increases the charge collection from CsPbBr3 crystals. Furthermore, The SEM mapping characterization (Fig. S4) of TiO2/CQDs film after spin-coated with DMF shows that the CQDs have a homogeneous distribution on TiO2 film without aggregations in spite of the similar DMF solvent for further spin-coating deposition of PbBr2 layer (Fig. S10).

Fig. 3 a describes the characteristic J-V curves of the pristine, PQDs-only and CQDs/PQDs all-inorganic PSCs and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The pristine PSC free of QDs achieves a PCE of 6.05 ± 0.39% (V oc = 1.23 ± 0.026 V, J sc = 6.44 ± 0.69 mA cm−2, FF = 76.4 ± 3.47%). By modifying CsPbBr3/carbon interface with PQDs to set an intermediate energy, the PCE, V oc and J sc are enhanced to 6.71 ± 0.19%, 1.31 ± 0.03 V and 6.55 ± 0.23 mA cm−2, respectively. Further modification of TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface with CQDs, the CQDs/PQDs PSC yields a PCE as high as 7.93 ± 0.29% (V oc = 1.45 ± 0.022 V, J sc = 7.24 ± 0.5 mA cm−2). The V oc value attained by the all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs with CQDs and PQDs is higher than the devices in previous reports [15,16]. The increased V oc mainly results from the suppressed charge recombination at perovskite/TiO2 interface and perovskite/carbon interface owing to the introduction of intermediate energy levels [32]. As for the decrease of FF can be ascribed to the introduce of CQDs film, causing an increase in the series resistance (R s) in devices (Figs. S5a and b) [33]. The photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) and integrated J sc curves are performed to cross-check the photoelectric performances, as shown in Fig. 3b. In comparison with state-of-the-art organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs, the pristine CsPbBr3 solar cell exhibits narrow light harvesting due to the large bandgap (2.3 eV) of CsPbBr3 halide. In a wavelength region of 300–550 nm, maximal IPCE value is ∼70% for referenced device, and it increases to 75% and 90% by interfacial modification with PQDs-only and CQDs/PQDs, respectively. The mechanism behind this enhancement is efficient electron extraction by CQDs and hole extraction by PQDs. Since the intermediated energy levels markedly reduce energy loss and space charge accumulation, therefore photo-induced charges prefer to separate instead of participating recombination processes. Notably, their integrated J sc values are measured to be 6.18, 6.34 and 7.25 mA cm−2 through overlap integral of λ P in IPCE/1240, where λ is wavelength and P in is incident intensity. And the integrated J sc values are in a good agreement with the measured J sc from photovoltaic tests.

Apart from improved efficiency, these inorganic PSCs have high reproducibility within ±5%, as shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S7, ten random devices are applied to compare the increasing tendency of photovoltaic parameters. Furthermore, the steady-state photo-current outputs for characterizing the reliability of J-V measurements are provided in Fig. 3d. It can be seen that a steady-state current density of 7.14 mA cm−2 and cell efficiency of 7.86% are obtained for optimized PSCs (measured at bias voltage of 1.1 V), which are both larger than that of QDs-free PSCs with a steady-state current density of 5.13 mA cm−2 and PCE of 5.73% (measured at bias voltage of 1.13 V). The deviation between PCEs obtained from steady-state and dynamic-state characterizations is mainly attributed to the existences of hysteresis in the devices (Fig. S5) [34–39].

According to above-mentioned discussion, the performance enhancement of PSCs are attributable to promoted charge extraction at CsPbBr3/charge-transporting material interface. In order to confirm the limited charge recombination behaviors, the J sc and V oc for inorganic PSCs with and without QDs as a function of light intensity are plotted in Fig. 4 a and b. According to J sc ∝ I
α (α ≤ 1), where I represents light intensity and α is an exponential factor to evaluate charge recombination [40]. A α value closer to 1 means the minimal carrier recombination for maximum carrier sweep out at the short circuit [41–43]. By fitting plots with linear relationships, the α is determined to be 0.907 for referenced PSC, while it increases to 0.924 and 0.998 upon interfacial modification with PQDs-only and CQDs/PQDs, respectively. Additionally, the V oc versus light intensity obeys Shockley-Read-Hall recombination mechanism [42], and the slope representing bimolecular recombination during charge transport deviates from (kT/e) [44], where k, T and e are the Boltzmann constant, the temperature in Kelvin, and the elementary charge, respectively. A slope as high as 2.23 kT/e is obtained for referenced PSC, and it reduces to 1.75 kT/e for PQDs-only modified device and 1.68 kT/e for CQDs/PQDs solar cell. Both investigations on J sc and V oc demonstrate a suppressed charge recombination by setting intermediated energy levels at interfaces.

Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and the time-resolved PL (TRPL) are measured under the same condition to evaluate charge extraction ability of PSCs. As shown in Fig. 4c, the PL peak intensity at 530 nm, which is consistent with previous reports [16,45], is quenched for CsPbBr3/PQDs film. A diminished PL intensity is likely to be the faster hole transport from CsPbBr3 light-harvesting layer. Fluorescence quenching is even more pronounced after simultaneously decorated by CQDs and PQDs. Moreover, TRPL decay curves depicted in Fig. 4d are used to calculate electron lifetime according to the single-exponential decay function I = Ae −(t-t
0 )/τ1, where I is PL intensity at time t, A is a constant, τ 1 is the faster component of the exciton spontaneous radiative recombination time [46]. The FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3 device exhibits an electron lifetime of about 3.98 ns, and it is prolonged to 5.83 ns for FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/PQDs due to the strengthened hole injection rate by PQDs. Once illumination, the photoexcited holes will rapidly transfer to carbon through the intermediate energy levels of PQDs, which reduces the opportunity of recombination reaction at CsPbBr3/carbon interface. Similarly, the matching interfacial energy level from CQDs is beneficial for election injection from perovskite to TiO2, therefore the electron lifetime for FTO/TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3 reduces to minimized 1.19 ns. All of these spectral characterizations indicate that the interfacial modification by CQDs and PQDs promotes charge extraction and are in favour of photovoltaic performance enhancement [47].

High efficiency and improved stability are equally important in promoting commercial applications of all-inorganic PSCs [48–50]. Two devices including pristine and CQDs/PQDs tailored CsPbBr3 solar cells are stored in 80% RH atmosphere, and the normalized PCEs are plotted in Fig. 5 . Due to the hydrophobicity of carbon back-electrode, the solar cells are expected to have high tolerance to high humidity. 95% of initial efficiency is remained by storing pristine PSC over 1400 h, while the CQDs/PQDs tailored device achieves a retention ratio as high as 99%. This result demonstrates a superior operational stability of all-inorganic PSCs and the addition of interfacial modification materials has a negligible impact on solar cell life.

By addressing large energy differences and serious charge recombination at TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces, we present here all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells modified with CQDs and PQDs, respectively. Arising from matching intermediate energy levels and promoted charge extraction, the CQDs/PQDs tailored PSC achieves a maximized PCE of 7.93% in comparison to 6.05% for pristine device. Furthermore, the all-inorganic PSCs have champion long-term stability over 1400 h under persistent attack by 80% humidity. Although this work is far from optimization, the preliminary results inspire us to develop high-performance PSCs with high efficiency and improved stability by interfacial engineering.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202, 61774139), and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM201702).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-purity perovskite films for solar cells with 9.72% efficiency
